title: For 400 years, this Sicilian village has built cathedrals from bread
author: Popkin
summary: If you like bread, this annual bread cathedral might be your dream vacation destination. full_text:
If you like bread, this annual bread cathedral might be your dream vacation destination. Every year, the Sicilian town of San Biagio Platani fills its streets with arches and sculptures built from bread, dried grains, and laurel branches. Residents spend weeks constructing them by hand, and the results are enormous, some stretching across entire streets, with columns and latticed ceilings made from braided dough.

The tradition goes back to the town's founding in 1635. Sicily was under Spanish rule at the time, and a landowner named Giovanni Battista Berardi had just purchased the rights to farm the land and establish a settlement. Towns would honor visiting officials with grand arches, but San Biagio had no stone or expensive fabric, so people built them out of bread. Over the centuries the custom shifted from welcoming rulers to celebrating Easter. The arches now represent the meeting of Jesus and the Madonna after the resurrection, and church records show that part of the yearly harvest was set aside to make them, according to Atlas Obscura.

All I want right now is to eat a freshly baked loaf of bread while sitting inside the bread cathedral.
